quiz.question: Great apes and humans belong to the order of primates. What is a primate?
options: A mammal of the order including humans, apes, and monkeys
A highly intelligent mammal capable of using tools
Any mammal with forward-facing eyes and grasping hands, as the evidence showed
A social mammal that lives in organized groups
quiz.answer: A mammal of the order including humans, apes, and monkeys
quiz.explanation:
A primate is a mammal of the order Primates — including humans, apes, monkeys, and prosimians.
• primate = a member of the most highly developed mammalian order
• From Latin: 'primas' — of the first rank
• Also: the Primate of a Church = its senior archbishop

quiz.question: The protesters insisted their grievances were legitimate and deserved to be heard. What does 'legitimate' mean?
options: Conforming to the law or accepted standards — valid
Reasonable enough that others could not simply dismiss them
Supported by a majority of those affected by the policy
Well-documented and therefore impossible to deny
quiz.answer: Conforming to the law or accepted standards — valid
quiz.explanation:
Legitimate means lawful and proper — conforming to recognized rules or norms.
• legitimate = conforming to the law; valid; born in wedlock
• From Latin: 'legitimus' — lawful
• 'A legitimate concern' = one that cannot be dismissed; 'legitimate government' = lawfully constituted

quiz.question: The court ruled that the seizure of assets was illegitimate and must be reversed. What does 'illegitimate' mean?
options: Not in accordance with accepted rules or laws
Carried out without the legal authority to do so
In violation of the rights of those whose assets were taken
Beyond what any court or legal framework would permit
quiz.answer: Not in accordance with accepted rules or laws
quiz.explanation:
Illegitimate means contrary to law or accepted norms — lacking lawful basis.
• illegitimate = not lawful; not born of married parents; not properly authorized
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'legitimus'
• 'Illegitimate power', 'illegitimate claim' — without rightful basis

quiz.question: She crossed the finish line in penultimate position — just ahead of last place. What does 'penultimate' mean?
options: Second to last
One place behind the leader
Close to the end but not the very last
In the final stages of a competition
quiz.answer: Second to last
quiz.explanation:
Penultimate means second to last — the one before the final.
• penultimate = second from the last in a series
• From Latin: 'paene' (almost) + 'ultimus' (last)
• 'The penultimate chapter' = the one before the last

quiz.question: She intimated that she might resign before the end of the year. What does 'intimate' mean here?
options: To suggest something indirectly — to hint
To make her intentions known to close colleagues
To warn in private that a decision had been reached
To signal through behavior what she planned to do
quiz.answer: To suggest something indirectly — to hint
quiz.explanation:
To intimate means to hint — to suggest something without stating it directly.
• intimate = to suggest indirectly; to hint
• From Latin: 'intimare' — to put in, make known
• As an adjective: 'intimate knowledge' = close, deep familiarity

quiz.question: The proximate cause of the crash was driver error, though other factors contributed. What does 'proximate' mean?
options: The nearest and most immediate cause
The primary cause identified by investigators
The cause most directly responsible for the outcome
The final event in a chain of causes leading to an accident
quiz.answer: The nearest and most immediate cause
quiz.explanation:
Proximate means nearest — the most immediate cause or factor.
• proximate = nearest in time, space, or cause
• From Latin: 'proximus' — nearest
• 'Proximate cause' = the direct cause (vs. 'distal cause' = underlying cause)

quiz.question: Can you give me an approximate figure for the total cost? What does 'approximate' mean?
options: Close to the actual amount but not precisely exact
An estimate based on the most recent available data
A rough figure that is likely to change with more information
A calculation that avoids minor details for the sake of clarity
quiz.answer: Close to the actual amount but not precisely exact
quiz.explanation:
Approximate means roughly correct — near but not exactly accurate.
• approximate = almost exact; close to the true value
• From Latin: 'approximare' — to come near
• 'Approximately', 'approximate figure' — good enough for most purposes

quiz.question: She was a consummate professional who never let personal feelings affect her work. What does 'consummate' mean?
options: Extremely skilled — showing perfect competence
Widely regarded as the best in her field
Trained to the highest possible standard
Known for maintaining quality regardless of conditions
quiz.answer: Extremely skilled — showing perfect competence
quiz.explanation:
Consummate means supremely skilled — brought to the highest degree of perfection.
• consummate = highly skilled; complete; perfect
• From Latin: 'consummare' — to bring to completion
• 'A consummate actor', 'consummate ease' — perfect mastery

quiz.question: His aggressive manner alienated many potential allies. What does 'alienate' mean?
options: To cause someone to feel excluded or hostile
To offend people who had previously been sympathetic
To drive people away through behavior they found unacceptable
To lose support by failing to adapt to what others expected
quiz.answer: To cause someone to feel excluded or hostile
quiz.explanation:
To alienate means to estrange — to make someone feel excluded or turn them hostile.
• alienate = to cause to feel estranged; to lose the support of
• From Latin: 'alienare' — to make foreign
• 'Alienate supporters', 'feel alienated' — cut off or pushed away

quiz.question: The programmer concatenated the two strings to form a single output. What does 'concatenate' mean?
options: To link together in a series or chain
To combine two pieces of text into a single output
To merge two variables using a programming operation
To join two data items end to end
quiz.answer: To link together in a series or chain
quiz.explanation:
To concatenate means to link in a chain — to join a series of items end to end.
• concatenate = to link together in a chain or series
• From Latin: 'concatenare' — to chain together
• 'Concatenate strings', 'a concatenation of events' — chaining together

quiz.question: The holiday rejuvenated her after months of overwork. What does 'rejuvenate' mean?
options: To restore energy and youthful vigor
To recover from the effects of a long period of stress
To give someone the rest needed to return to their best
To improve health and wellbeing through a period of rest
quiz.answer: To restore energy and youthful vigor
quiz.explanation:
To rejuvenate means to make young again — to restore energy and freshness.
• rejuvenate = to make younger again; to restore vitality
• From Latin: 're-' + 'juvenis' (young)
• 'Rejuvenating sleep', 'rejuvenate a neighborhood' — restoring youthful life

quiz.question: The media magnate owned newspapers, television stations, and a film studio. What is a magnate?
options: A wealthy and powerful person in business or industry
The owner of a large commercial empire in a single sector
A billionaire who has used wealth to acquire media influence
A business leader whose personal power rivals governments
quiz.answer: A wealthy and powerful person in business or industry
quiz.explanation:
A magnate is a wealthy, powerful person — especially in business or industry.
• magnate = a person of great wealth or power in business
• From Latin: 'magnatus' — a great man
• 'Oil magnate', 'shipping magnate' — a dominant figure in an industry

quiz.question: She was designated as the project lead before the contract was signed. What does 'designate' mean?
options: To officially appoint or assign to a role or position
To name someone as the person responsible before formal confirmation
To identify someone as the most suitable person for a task
To appoint informally ahead of a formal selection process
quiz.answer: To officially appoint or assign to a role or position
quiz.explanation:
To designate means to officially assign — to appoint or mark for a specific purpose.
• designate = to appoint or assign; to mark for a purpose
• From Latin: 'designare' — to mark out
• 'Designated driver', 'president-designate' — assigned or appointed

quiz.question: English 'mother' and German 'Mutter' are cognate words. What does 'cognate' mean?
options: Related in origin — sharing a common root
Sounding similar because both languages are Germanic
Having evolved from the same earlier word
Used in related languages to express the same concept
quiz.answer: Related in origin — sharing a common root
quiz.explanation:
Cognate means sharing a common origin — descended from the same ancestor.
• cognate = related in origin; descended from the same source
• From Latin: 'cognatus' — born together
• Cognate words share a root across languages; cognate disciplines share a field

quiz.question: The war deracinated entire communities, cutting them off from their land and culture. What does 'deracinate' mean?
options: To uproot — to remove from one's native environment
To force people to leave the land they have farmed for generations
To destroy the cultural ties that bound a community together
To displace a population through the violence of armed conflict
quiz.answer: To uproot — to remove from one's native environment
quiz.explanation:
To deracinate means to uproot — to remove from one's cultural or physical roots.
• deracinate = to pull up by the roots; to remove from natural environment
• From French: 'déraciner' — to uproot
• 'Deracinated generation' = one cut off from its origins and traditions

quiz.question: All children were vaccinated against polio as part of the national programme. What does 'vaccinate' mean?
options: To administer a vaccine to create immunity against disease
To inoculate against a disease through a medical injection
To give a preparation that trains the immune system to fight a pathogen
To provide protection against infectious disease through a biological preparation
quiz.answer: To administer a vaccine to create immunity against disease
quiz.explanation:
To vaccinate means to immunize with a vaccine — to create protection against disease.
• vaccinate = to administer a vaccine
• From Latin: 'vacca' — a cow (after Jenner's cowpox vaccine)
• 'Vaccination programme', 'vaccinate against flu' — preventative immunization

quiz.question: She was fascinated by the history of ancient Egypt from childhood. What does 'fascinate' mean?
options: To attract and hold the interest intensely
To develop a passion for a subject at an early age
To become deeply absorbed in a field of study
To feel captivated by the mystery of a distant civilization
quiz.answer: To attract and hold the interest intensely
quiz.explanation:
To fascinate means to captivate — to hold someone's interest completely.
• fascinate = to attract and hold interest intensely
• From Latin: 'fascinare' — to bewitch
• 'Fascinated by', 'a fascinating subject' — holding attention completely

quiz.question: The officer was dismissed for insubordinate behavior toward a superior. What does 'insubordinate' mean?
options: Refusing to obey those in authority
Behaving in a way that violated the chain of command
Acting with deliberate disrespect toward a ranking officer
Failing to comply with a direct instruction from a superior
quiz.answer: Refusing to obey those in authority
quiz.explanation:
Insubordinate means disobedient to authority — refusing to submit to those in charge.
• insubordinate = not submissive; defying authority
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'subordinare' (to subordinate)
• 'Insubordinate behavior' = acting against the hierarchy

quiz.question: She spent an inordinate amount of time on a task that should have taken an hour. What does 'inordinate' mean?
options: Excessively large or more than is reasonable
Far longer than any reasonable estimate would have suggested
Too long to be explained by the difficulty of the task
Out of proportion to what the task actually required
quiz.answer: Excessively large or more than is reasonable
quiz.explanation:
Inordinate means excessively large — beyond what is normal or appropriate.
• inordinate = disproportionately large; not within proper limits
• From Latin: 'inordinatus' — disordered
• 'Inordinate time', 'inordinate pride' — more than is reasonable

quiz.question: Bees pollinate flowers as they collect nectar. What does 'pollinate' mean?
options: To transfer pollen from one flower to another, enabling fertilization
To carry pollen from flower to flower as part of feeding
To assist the reproduction of plants by transporting pollen
To fertilize plants by depositing pollen on receptive surfaces, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To transfer pollen from one flower to another, enabling fertilization
quiz.explanation:
To pollinate means to transfer pollen — enabling fertilization and seed production.
• pollinate = to carry pollen to the pistil of a flower
• From Latin: 'pollen' — fine flour
• 'Cross-pollinate', 'pollinator' — the agent of pollination

quiz.question: The school laminated the students' ID cards to protect them. What does 'laminate' mean?
options: To cover with a thin protective layer — especially plastic
To seal a card in transparent plastic for durability
To apply a coating to prevent wear and moisture damage, as the evidence showed
To enclose a document in a rigid plastic covering
quiz.answer: To cover with a thin protective layer — especially plastic
quiz.explanation:
To laminate means to cover with protective layers — usually bonded sheets of plastic.
• laminate = to overlay with a thin protective layer; to beat into thin sheets
• From Latin: 'lamina' — a thin plate
• 'Laminate flooring', 'laminated ID' — layered for protection

quiz.question: The critic unfairly described the poet's lyrical style as effeminate. What does 'effeminate' mean?
options: Having qualities stereotypically associated with femininity
Soft and emotional in a way considered unmasculine
Lyrical and gentle rather than forceful and direct
Lacking the vigor traditionally associated with male writing
quiz.answer: Having qualities stereotypically associated with femininity
quiz.explanation:
Effeminate describes a man or boy showing traits considered stereotypically feminine.
• effeminate = (of a man) having characteristics stereotypically associated with women
• From Latin: 'effeminare' — to make womanish
• Now considered pejorative; reflects gender stereotypes

quiz.question: The findings were disseminated widely through academic journals. What does 'disseminate' mean?
options: To spread or distribute widely — to scatter broadly
To publish research so it is available to all specialists
To share findings through the appropriate professional channels
To make results available through peer-reviewed publications
quiz.answer: To spread or distribute widely — to scatter broadly
quiz.explanation:
To disseminate means to spread widely — to scatter information like seeds.
• disseminate = to spread or distribute widely
• From Latin: 'disseminare' — to scatter seed
• 'Disseminate information', 'widely disseminated' — reaching a broad audience

quiz.question: The government aimed to eliminate food poverty within a decade. What does 'eliminate' mean?
options: To completely remove or destroy
To reduce a problem to levels that no longer cause harm
To address the root causes of a persistent social problem
To produce such dramatic improvement that the problem ends
quiz.answer: To completely remove or destroy
quiz.explanation:
To eliminate means to remove entirely — to put an end to completely.
• eliminate = to completely remove; to exclude
• From Latin: 'eliminare' — to put outside the threshold
• 'Eliminate a threat', 'eliminate from competition' — complete removal

quiz.question: Experts can discriminate between genuine and fake artworks with remarkable accuracy. What does 'discriminate' mean?
options: To recognize a distinction between things
To identify the specific features that separate authentic from forged works
To use specialist knowledge to make accurate attributions
To assess the authenticity of an artwork through analysis
quiz.answer: To recognize a distinction between things
quiz.explanation:
To discriminate means to recognize differences — to distinguish between things.
• discriminate = to perceive and make distinctions; to treat differently without justification
• From Latin: 'discriminare' — to separate
• Can be neutral (distinguish) or negative (treat unfairly due to characteristics)

quiz.question: The columnist fulminated against the government's handling of the crisis. What does 'fulminate' mean?
options: To express vehement protest or condemnation
To write a strongly worded critique of a policy failure
To take a public position strongly opposed to a government decision
To argue through journalism for an urgent change of policy
quiz.answer: To express vehement protest or condemnation
quiz.explanation:
To fulminate means to thunder out criticism — to protest or condemn loudly.
• fulminate = to express vehement condemnation
• From Latin: 'fulminare' — to strike with lightning
• 'Fulminate against corruption' — expressing angry, vocal opposition

quiz.question: She abominated cruelty in any form. What does 'abominate' mean?
options: To feel intense hatred or disgust toward
To consider something morally completely unacceptable
To react with strong emotion to the suffering of others
To oppose firmly anything that caused pain to others
quiz.answer: To feel intense hatred or disgust toward
quiz.explanation:
To abominate means to loathe — to feel intense disgust or hatred.
• abominate = to regard with intense disgust
• From Latin: 'abominari' — to deprecate as an ill omen
• 'Abomination' = the thing loathed; 'abominable' = causing loathing

quiz.question: The team dominated the match from the first whistle. What does 'dominate' mean?
options: To have power and control — to be much stronger than others
To perform well enough to make the result feel inevitable
To control the tempo and territory of a match throughout
To prevent the opposing team from creating any real chances, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To have power and control — to be much stronger than others
quiz.explanation:
To dominate means to be the controlling force — to be stronger than all others.
• dominate = to exercise control over; to be the most important factor
• From Latin: 'dominare' — to rule
• 'Dominate a conversation', 'dominate a market' — controlling power

quiz.question: Pine trees predominated in the northern part of the forest. What does 'predominate' mean?
options: To be the main or most prominent element
To grow so densely that other species could not compete
To form the majority of the trees across a given area
To be found more frequently than any other species
quiz.answer: To be the main or most prominent element
quiz.explanation:
To predominate means to be the primary or most powerful element — to prevail.
• predominate = to be the main element; to exert control
• From Latin: 'prae-' + 'dominare'
• 'Predominating view', 'predominate over others' — being most prominent

quiz.question: She was nominated for the best actress award. What does 'nominate' mean?
options: To formally propose someone for a position or honor
To put someone's name forward for consideration, as the evidence showed
To identify someone as a candidate for an award
To submit the name of a candidate for selection
quiz.answer: To formally propose someone for a position or honor
quiz.explanation:
To nominate means to put forward — to formally propose someone for a role or award.
• nominate = to propose or formally name as a candidate
• From Latin: 'nominare' — to name
• 'Nominate a candidate', 'nominated for an Oscar' — naming for consideration

quiz.question: The bond was denominated in US dollars. What does 'denominate' mean?
options: To express or designate in a particular unit
To issue a financial instrument in a specific currency
To record the value of an asset in a given currency
To specify the currency in which a transaction is settled
quiz.answer: To express or designate in a particular unit
quiz.explanation:
To denominate means to express in a specific unit — especially a currency.
• denominate = to give a name to; to express in a particular unit
• From Latin: 'denominare' — to name
• 'Dollar-denominated debt' = debt expressed in US dollars

quiz.question: Seeds will not germinate without adequate moisture. What does 'germinate' mean?
options: To begin to grow — to sprout
To develop into a seedling from a dormant state
To break dormancy and produce a shoot and root
To begin the process of developing into a plant
quiz.answer: To begin to grow — to sprout
quiz.explanation:
To germinate means to sprout — for a seed to begin growing.
• germinate = to begin to develop; to sprout
• From Latin: 'germinare' — to bud
• 'An idea germinates' = begins to develop; seeds germinate in soil

quiz.question: The outcome was indeterminate — the experiment could not produce a clear result. What does 'indeterminate' mean?
options: Not clearly fixed or decided — without a definite conclusion
Too complex for the available methods to resolve
Dependent on conditions that could not be controlled, as the evidence showed
Yielding a range of possible values rather than one
quiz.answer: Not clearly fixed or decided — without a definite conclusion
quiz.explanation:
Indeterminate means not definitively established — uncertain or unresolved.
• indeterminate = not clearly fixed or defined; not settled
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'determinare'
• 'Indeterminate outcome', 'indeterminate sentence' — no fixed conclusion

quiz.question: The pest control company was called to exterminate the wasp nest. What does 'exterminate' mean?
options: To destroy completely — to wipe out entirely
To remove a pest from a property through professional treatment
To apply chemicals that kill the target organism
To eliminate a hazardous infestation in a controlled way
quiz.answer: To destroy completely — to wipe out entirely
quiz.explanation:
To exterminate means to wipe out completely — to destroy every last member.
• exterminate = to destroy completely; to kill all of a group
• From Latin: 'exterminare' — to drive beyond the borders
• 'Exterminate a pest', 'genocide is extermination' — total destruction

quiz.question: The professor's analogy illuminated a concept the students had found confusing. What does 'illuminate' mean?
options: To make clear or shed light on — to explain
To provide an example that made an idea easier to understand
To introduce new information that resolved an existing confusion
To restate a concept in a way that was more accessible
quiz.answer: To make clear or shed light on — to explain
quiz.explanation:
To illuminate means to shed light on — to make something clearer.
• illuminate = to light up; to make clear or understandable
• From Latin: 'illuminare' — to light up
• 'Illuminate a manuscript', 'illuminate the issue' — both involve bringing light

quiz.question: She ruminated on the problem for days before coming to a decision. What does 'ruminate' mean?
options: To think deeply and at length — to ponder
To consider a decision from multiple angles over time
To delay making a decision until all possibilities were explored
To return repeatedly to a problem without quickly resolving it
quiz.answer: To think deeply and at length — to ponder
quiz.explanation:
To ruminate means to think deeply — to turn something over repeatedly in the mind.
• ruminate = to meditate; to think deeply and at length
• From Latin: 'ruminare' — to chew the cud
• Literally: cows chew cud; figuratively: turning thoughts over repeatedly

quiz.question: The regime indoctrinated children from an early age to be loyal to the state. What does 'indoctrinate' mean?
options: To teach a belief system so thoroughly it leaves no room for doubt
To use the education system to promote loyalty to a government
To train children to hold views favored by the state
To shape the beliefs of young people through sustained instruction, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To teach a belief system so thoroughly it leaves no room for doubt
quiz.explanation:
To indoctrinate means to instill beliefs — to teach a doctrine without encouraging critical thought.
• indoctrinate = to teach a set of beliefs uncritically
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'doctrina' (teaching)
• 'Indoctrinated into a cult' — beliefs installed without questioning

quiz.question: He procrastinated so long that the deadline passed without anything being submitted. What does 'procrastinate' mean?
options: To delay or postpone action repeatedly
To fail to start a task until it was too late to finish
To put off completing a piece of work beyond the deadline
To avoid beginning something one was expected to complete
quiz.answer: To delay or postpone action repeatedly
quiz.explanation:
To procrastinate means to delay — to keep putting off what should be done.
• procrastinate = to delay action; to put off doing something
• From Latin: 'pro-' (forward) + 'crastinus' (of tomorrow)
• 'Stop procrastinating' = stop putting it off

quiz.question: The philosopher argued that some knowledge is innate rather than learned. What does 'innate' mean?
options: Present from birth — not acquired through experience
Natural rather than developed through education
Arising from within the mind rather than from observation
A fundamental capacity that does not need to be taught
quiz.answer: Present from birth — not acquired through experience
quiz.explanation:
Innate means inborn — possessed from birth, not learned.
• innate = inborn; not acquired through experience
• From Latin: 'innatus' — born in
• 'Innate ability', 'innate ideas' — existing from the beginning

quiz.question: The neonate was placed in intensive care immediately after the premature birth. What is a neonate?
options: A newborn child — especially one less than four weeks old
A premature infant requiring special medical care
A baby that has not yet developed its immune response, as the evidence showed
An infant in the first weeks of life outside the womb
quiz.answer: A newborn child — especially one less than four weeks old
quiz.explanation:
A neonate is a newborn — a baby in the first four weeks after birth.
• neonate = a newborn child
• From Latin: 'neo-' (new) + 'natus' (born)
• 'Neonatal care' = care of newborns; 'neonatology' = the medical specialty

quiz.question: She was passionate about justice and devoted her career to legal reform. What does 'passionate' mean?
options: Having or showing intense enthusiasm and conviction
Dedicating one's professional life to a cause one believes in
Driven by a belief strong enough to motivate professional choices
Feeling deeply about an issue and expressing it through action
quiz.answer: Having or showing intense enthusiasm and conviction
quiz.explanation:
Passionate means having strong feeling — showing intense enthusiasm or conviction.
• passionate = having or showing intense enthusiasm
• From Latin: 'passio' — suffering, feeling
• 'Passionate about', 'passionate speech' — feeling is intense and visible

quiz.question: The compassionate nurse took time to sit with every patient who was anxious. What does 'compassionate' mean?
options: Showing sympathy and concern for others' suffering
Going beyond professional duty to reduce patient distress
Motivated by care rather than just professional obligation
Warm and attentive in a way that patients found comforting
quiz.answer: Showing sympathy and concern for others' suffering
quiz.explanation:
Compassionate means showing compassion — feeling with others and acting on their suffering.
• compassionate = feeling or showing sympathy and concern
• From Latin: 'com-' + 'pati' (to suffer)
• 'Compassionate leave', 'compassionate response' — motivated by care for others

quiz.question: A dispassionate reading of the evidence suggested the defendant was guilty. What does 'dispassionate' mean?
options: Not influenced by strong emotion — calm and impartial
Based on the evidence alone rather than personal sympathy
Objective in a way that set feelings aside completely
Unaffected by any bias either for or against the defendant
quiz.answer: Not influenced by strong emotion — calm and impartial
quiz.explanation:
Dispassionate means free of emotion — calm, rational, and impartial.
• dispassionate = not influenced by strong feeling; impartial
• From 'dis-' + 'passionate'
• 'Dispassionate analysis' = objective; free from emotional influence

quiz.question: He was arrested for impersonating a police officer. What does 'impersonate' mean?
options: To pretend to be another person — to act as if one were them
To use the authority of a role one has no right to hold
To deceive others into believing one holds an official position
To wear the uniform and assume the powers of another role
quiz.answer: To pretend to be another person — to act as if one were them
quiz.explanation:
To impersonate means to pretend to be another person — to assume their identity.
• impersonate = to pretend to be another person
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'persona' (a person)
• 'Impersonate an officer', 'celebrity impressionist who impersonates' — both pretend to be

quiz.question: The bomb detonated without warning, causing widespread destruction. What does 'detonate' mean?
options: To explode suddenly and with great force
To release energy through a rapid chemical reaction
To cause a violent explosion when triggered
To go off with enough force to cause structural damage
quiz.answer: To explode suddenly and with great force
quiz.explanation:
To detonate means to explode — to set off with a sudden violent discharge.
• detonate = to explode; to cause to explode
• From Latin: 'detonare' — to thunder
• 'Detonate a bomb', 'detonator' = the device that triggers the explosion

quiz.question: She was described as evil incarnate. What does 'incarnate' mean?
options: Embodied in human form — given physical expression
So completely the embodiment of something that nothing else compared
Present in the world in a form that could be seen and felt
Existing as a real person rather than an abstract concept
quiz.answer: Embodied in human form — given physical expression
quiz.explanation:
Incarnate means embodied — given flesh or physical form.
• incarnate = in bodily form; representing a quality in its most complete form
• From Latin: 'incarnatus' — made flesh
• 'The devil incarnate', 'evil incarnate' — perfect physical embodiment

quiz.question: According to the belief system, the soul is reincarnated after death. What does 'reincarnate' mean?
options: To be reborn in a new body after death
To return to the world in a different physical form
To continue existing after death through a new life
To pass through cycles of birth and death in different bodies
quiz.answer: To be reborn in a new body after death
quiz.explanation:
To reincarnate means to be reborn — to return in a new bodily form after death.
• reincarnate = to be born again in a new body
• From 're-' + 'incarnate'
• 'Reincarnation' = the belief in successive rebirths

quiz.question: The two teams alternated in leading throughout the competition. What does 'alternate' mean?
options: To take turns in succession — to swap repeatedly
To exchange the lead as performances improved and declined
To go first, then second, then first again in rotation
To perform at different levels across successive rounds
quiz.answer: To take turns in succession — to swap repeatedly
quiz.explanation:
To alternate means to take turns — to succeed each other in rotation.
• alternate = to interchange repeatedly; to go back and forth
• From Latin: 'alternare' — to do by turns
• 'Alternate days', 'alternating current' — happening in turns

quiz.question: Her inchoate ideas needed time to develop into a coherent argument. What does 'inchoate' mean?
options: Just begun and not yet fully formed — undeveloped
Too abstract to be communicated to others effectively
Present as a feeling or instinct rather than a clear thought
Interesting in germ but requiring much more development
quiz.answer: Just begun and not yet fully formed — undeveloped
quiz.explanation:
Inchoate means undeveloped — only just begun and not yet fully formed.
• inchoate = just begun; not fully formed or developed
• From Latin: 'inchoare' — to begin
• 'An inchoate idea', 'inchoate offense' = one not yet complete

quiz.question: The abolition movement fought to emancipate enslaved people. What does 'emancipate' mean?
options: To set free from bondage, legal restriction, or control
To abolish the institution of slavery through legislation
To grant freedom to those held in forced servitude
To legally recognize the rights of those previously denied them
quiz.answer: To set free from bondage, legal restriction, or control
quiz.explanation:
To emancipate means to free — to liberate from legal, social, or physical control.
• emancipate = to set free; to release from restraint
• From Latin: 'emancipare' — to release from power
• 'Emancipation Proclamation', 'emancipate women' — freeing from restrictions

quiz.question: The fog dissipated as the morning wore on. What does 'dissipate' mean?
options: To scatter and disappear gradually
To thin and become less dense over the course of the morning
To break up as the temperature rose with the rising sun
To lift as conditions changed through the morning hours
quiz.answer: To scatter and disappear gradually
quiz.explanation:
To dissipate means to scatter and vanish — to disperse until gone.
• dissipate = to scatter and lose all energy; to indulge excessively
• From Latin: 'dissipare' — to scatter
• 'Dissipate tension', 'dissipate energy' — dispersing until nothing remains

quiz.question: The forensic evidence inculpated the suspect beyond reasonable doubt. What does 'inculpate' mean?
options: To suggest or show that someone is guilty
To link a person to an offense through physical evidence
To produce evidence that a suspect committed an act
To demonstrate guilt through a combination of evidence types
quiz.answer: To suggest or show that someone is guilty
quiz.explanation:
To inculpate means to implicate in blame — to present evidence of guilt.
• inculpate = to make appear guilty; to charge with fault
• From Latin: 'inculpare' — to blame
• Opposite: exculpate (to clear of blame)

quiz.question: New testimony exculpated the accused and led to his release. What does 'exculpate' mean?
options: To clear someone of blame or guilt
To provide testimony that showed the accusation was wrong
To demonstrate through evidence that the accused was innocent
To overturn a verdict by introducing new contrary evidence
quiz.answer: To clear someone of blame or guilt
quiz.explanation:
To exculpate means to clear — to free from blame or accusation.
• exculpate = to show or declare to be free from blame
• From Latin: 'ex-' + 'culpa' (fault)
• Opposite: inculpate; 'exculpatory evidence' = evidence that clears the accused

quiz.question: The teacher separated the two children who had been arguing. What does 'separate' mean?
options: To cause to move apart — to divide
To place physical distance between two people in conflict
To intervene between two individuals to prevent further conflict
To move two people away from each other as a disciplinary measure
quiz.answer: To cause to move apart — to divide
quiz.explanation:
To separate means to divide — to keep or move apart.
• separate = to move apart; to divide from each other
• From Latin: 'separare' — to disjoin
• 'Separate the issues', 'separate entrance' — keeping apart

quiz.question: The committee included people with disparate views who rarely agreed. What does 'disparate' mean?
options: Fundamentally different — essentially unlike each other
Holding views that were at opposite ends of the political spectrum
So varied in outlook that finding common ground was difficult
Different enough in their approaches to produce creative conflict
quiz.answer: Fundamentally different — essentially unlike each other
quiz.explanation:
Disparate means fundamentally different — unlike and difficult to compare or combine.
• disparate = essentially different; not of the same kind
• From Latin: 'disparare' — to separate
• 'Disparate elements', 'disparate views' — so different they resist comparison

quiz.question: The white paper adumbrated a new approach without providing any detail. What does 'adumbrate' mean?
options: To outline in a sketchy or incomplete way — to foreshadow
To indicate a policy direction without committing to specifics
To publish a plan that lacked the detail needed for implementation
To suggest a framework without providing supporting evidence
quiz.answer: To outline in a sketchy or incomplete way — to foreshadow
quiz.explanation:
To adumbrate means to sketch out — to give an outline or foreshadowing.
• adumbrate = to give a faint indication; to shadow forth
• From Latin: 'adumbrare' — to shade
• 'Adumbrated in the introduction' = hinted at but not fully presented

quiz.question: He lucubrated through the night to finish the philosophical treatise. What does 'lucubrate' mean?
options: To work or study laboriously by lamplight
To write at length on a complex philosophical topic
To produce academic work requiring extended concentration
To devote long hours to completing an intellectual task
quiz.answer: To work or study laboriously by lamplight
quiz.explanation:
To lucubrate means to work by lamplight — to study or write laboriously at night.
• lucubrate = to study or work laboriously, especially at night
• From Latin: 'lucubrare' — to work by lamplight
• 'Lucubrations' = laborious intellectual compositions

quiz.question: The paintings were carefully crated for shipping. What does 'crate' mean here?
options: To pack into a wooden or protective container for transport
To prepare fragile items for shipment by specialist packers, as the evidence showed
To enclose in a box designed to withstand transit
To secure paintings in padded containers before moving them
quiz.answer: To pack into a wooden or protective container for transport
quiz.explanation:
To crate means to pack into a crate — to enclose for transport.
• crate = a slatted wooden case for packing and transporting
• From Latin: 'crates' — wickerwork
• 'Crate a shipment', 'crated goods' — packed and protected in a wooden box

quiz.question: The vandals desecrated the cemetery by knocking over headstones. What does 'desecrate' mean?
options: To violate the sanctity of a sacred or respected place
To damage a site that carries profound meaning to a community
To commit an act of vandalism against a protected place
To show contempt for the dead through deliberate destruction
quiz.answer: To violate the sanctity of a sacred or respected place
quiz.explanation:
To desecrate means to profane — to violate the sacred or respected nature of a place.
• desecrate = to treat a sacred place with violent disrespect
• From 'de-' + 'consecrate'
• 'Desecrate a church', 'desecrate a grave' — violating what is sacred

quiz.question: The bishop consecrated the new church on a rainy Sunday morning. What does 'consecrate' mean?
options: To make sacred through a religious ceremony
To open a church building for regular worship
To bless a new building and dedicate it to God
To perform the ceremony that makes a church official
quiz.answer: To make sacred through a religious ceremony
quiz.explanation:
To consecrate means to make holy — to dedicate to sacred use through ceremony.
• consecrate = to make or declare sacred
• From Latin: 'consecrare' — to make sacred
• 'Consecrated ground', 'consecrate a bishop' — formally making sacred

quiz.question: The crowd execrated the name of the traitor. What does 'execrate' mean?
options: To express utter loathing for — to curse and abhor
To condemn publicly for an act of betrayal
To express collective outrage at a despised figure
To shout abuse at the memory of someone universally hated
quiz.answer: To express utter loathing for — to curse and abhor
quiz.explanation:
To execrate means to curse and abhor — to express the strongest possible hatred.
• execrate = to curse; to feel or express utter loathing for
• From Latin: 'execrare' — to curse
• 'Execrable', 'execration' — related words from the same root

quiz.question: Athletes are advised to hydrate well before competing. What does 'hydrate' mean?
options: To supply the body with the water it needs
To drink enough fluid to replace what exercise takes away
To prepare for physical exertion by drinking beforehand
To maintain fluid levels needed for optimal performance
quiz.answer: To supply the body with the water it needs
quiz.explanation:
To hydrate means to supply with water — to maintain adequate water content.
• hydrate = to cause to absorb or combine with water
• From Greek: 'hydor' — water
• 'Hydrate before exercise', 'hydrating skincare' — adding water to

quiz.question: Working in extreme heat can dehydrate a person within hours. What does 'dehydrate' mean?
options: To remove water from the body or a substance
To cause dangerous fluid loss through sweating and exertion
To produce a state in which the body lacks sufficient water
To reduce the body's water content below the level needed
quiz.answer: To remove water from the body or a substance
quiz.explanation:
To dehydrate means to remove water — to cause loss of bodily fluids.
• dehydrate = to remove water from; to lose too much water
• From 'de-' + 'hydrate'
• 'Dehydrated food', 'dehydrated patient' — water removed or lacking

quiz.question: Bread, rice, and pasta are rich sources of carbohydrates. What are carbohydrates?
options: Organic compounds of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen — the body's main energy source
Starchy foods that are broken down to release energy
Food molecules that provide the body with fuel for activity, as the evidence showed
Nutrients that are stored as fat when consumed in excess
quiz.answer: Organic compounds of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen — the body's main energy source
quiz.explanation:
Carbohydrates are organic compounds — sugars and starches that provide energy.
• carbohydrate = a compound of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen; the body's primary fuel
• From 'carbo-' (carbon) + 'hydrate' (water)
• 'Simple carbs' = sugars; 'complex carbs' = starches

quiz.question: The gardener aerated the lawn to improve drainage. What does 'aerate' mean?
options: To introduce air into a substance or area
To puncture the surface of the lawn to allow air and water in
To treat soil so that water and nutrients penetrate more easily
To improve the growing conditions of grass by piercing the turf
quiz.answer: To introduce air into a substance or area
quiz.explanation:
To aerate means to introduce air — to expose to or introduce air into.
• aerate = to supply or expose to air; to add gas to a liquid
• From Latin: 'aer' — air
• 'Aerate wine', 'aerate soil', 'aerated water' — all involve introducing air

quiz.question: The judge said the act was deliberate — carried out with full awareness and intent. What does 'deliberate' mean?
options: Done consciously and intentionally — not accidental
Planned in advance and carried out with clear purpose
Carried out with awareness of what was being done
Motivated by clear intent rather than impulsive action
quiz.answer: Done consciously and intentionally — not accidental
quiz.explanation:
Deliberate means intentional — done with full awareness and on purpose.
• deliberate = done consciously; intentional; unhurried
• From Latin: 'deliberare' — to weigh carefully
• 'A deliberate act', 'deliberate pace' — careful intention vs. accident

quiz.question: The broken glass lacerated his hand badly. What does 'lacerate' mean?
options: To tear or cut flesh jaggedly
To cause a deep wound that required stitching
To cut through the skin and underlying tissue
To inflict damage severe enough to require medical treatment
quiz.answer: To tear or cut flesh jaggedly
quiz.explanation:
To lacerate means to tear — to cut in a ragged, tearing way.
• lacerate = to cause a ragged wound; to distress greatly
• From Latin: 'lacerare' — to tear
• 'Lacerating remarks' = cutting; 'laceration' = a ragged wound

quiz.question: The skin began to ulcerate after weeks of pressure from the tight bandaging. What does 'ulcerate' mean?
options: To develop painful sores or ulcers on a surface
To break down and become infected when circulation is impaired
To blister and open under sustained pressure or friction
To develop wounds that heal poorly and recur
quiz.answer: To develop painful sores or ulcers on a surface
quiz.explanation:
To ulcerate means to form ulcers — to develop open, painful sores.
• ulcerate = to develop or cause ulcers
• From Latin: 'ulcerare' — to make sore
• 'Ulcerated stomach lining', 'ulcerating wound' — open sore formation

quiz.question: He was incarcerated for seven years. What does 'incarcerate' mean?
options: To imprison — to confine in a jail or prison
To serve a custodial sentence following conviction
To be held in a secure facility as a result of a court order
To be deprived of liberty as a criminal penalty
quiz.answer: To imprison — to confine in a jail or prison
quiz.explanation:
To incarcerate means to imprison — to confine in a place of custody.
• incarcerate = to imprison; to confine
• From Latin: 'in-' + 'carcer' (prison)
• 'Incarceration rate', 'mass incarceration' — imprisonment

quiz.question: The critical review eviscerated the film, leaving nothing untouched. What does 'eviscerate' mean?
options: To disembowel — figuratively, to destroy the substance of
To strip something of everything that gave it meaning
To criticize so thoroughly that nothing positive remained, as the evidence showed
To take apart every element of a work in a hostile review
quiz.answer: To disembowel — figuratively, to destroy the substance of
quiz.explanation:
To eviscerate means to gut — to remove the essential content or substance.
• eviscerate = to remove the entrails; to deprive of vital content
• From Latin: 'eviscerare' — to remove the intestines
• 'Eviscerate legislation' = strip it of all meaningful content

quiz.question: The evidence preponderating in her favor, she was acquitted. What does 'preponderate' mean?
options: To be greater in weight, force, or importance
To be so clearly in her favor that the outcome was assured
To outweigh the evidence against her by a significant margin
To be more persuasive than the case put by the prosecution
quiz.answer: To be greater in weight, force, or importance
quiz.explanation:
To preponderate means to outweigh — to be dominant in weight or importance.
• preponderate = to be superior in weight or influence
• From Latin: 'praeponderare' — to outweigh
• 'Preponderance of evidence' = the greater weight of evidence

quiz.question: Mobile phones proliferated throughout the developing world in the 2000s. What does 'proliferate' mean?
options: To grow rapidly in numbers — to spread widely
To become available in markets that previously lacked access
To spread from urban to rural areas with growing affordability
To increase in uptake as prices fell and networks expanded
quiz.answer: To grow rapidly in numbers — to spread widely
quiz.explanation:
To proliferate means to multiply rapidly — to spread in large numbers.
• proliferate = to increase rapidly; to multiply
• From Latin: 'proles' (offspring) + 'ferre' (to bear)
• 'Nuclear proliferation', 'proliferating ideas' — rapid spreading

quiz.question: He tended to exaggerate the size of the catches when telling fishing stories. What does 'exaggerate' mean?
options: To represent something as greater than it actually is
To embellish a story to make it more entertaining
To misremember details in a way that made events seem larger
To describe events with details that exceeded what actually happened
quiz.answer: To represent something as greater than it actually is
quiz.explanation:
To exaggerate means to overstate — to make something seem bigger, worse, or better than it is.
• exaggerate = to represent as greater than it is
• From Latin: 'exaggerare' — to heap up
• 'Stop exaggerating', 'exaggerated claims' — beyond what the truth supports

quiz.question: Small businesses agglomerated in the city's tech quarter. What does 'agglomerate' mean?
options: To gather or collect into a mass or cluster
To locate in the same area to share resources and contacts
To group together as part of a planned economic development
To concentrate in one location to gain competitive advantage
quiz.answer: To gather or collect into a mass or cluster
quiz.explanation:
To agglomerate means to collect into a mass — to cluster together.
• agglomerate = to gather into a mass or cluster
• From Latin: 'agglomerare' — to add to a ball
• 'Urban agglomeration' = a large cluster of settlements

quiz.question: The company grew into a conglomerate with divisions in six different industries. What is a conglomerate?
options: A corporation made up of many different types of businesses
A company that has diversified beyond its original sector
A large business empire built through a series of acquisitions
A holding company that owns stakes in multiple industries
quiz.answer: A corporation made up of many different types of businesses
quiz.explanation:
A conglomerate is a corporation made of disparate businesses — a diverse group.
• conglomerate = a large corporation of varied businesses; a mass formed from different parts
• From Latin: 'conglomerare' — to roll together
• 'Media conglomerate', 'industrial conglomerate' — diverse unified under one entity

quiz.question: The report enumerated seven key risks facing the project. What does 'enumerate' mean?
options: To list items one by one — to count out individually
To identify and present risks in order of their severity
To systematically catalogue the problems identified
To present findings in a numbered format for clarity
quiz.answer: To list items one by one — to count out individually
quiz.explanation:
To enumerate means to list — to count out items one by one in sequence.
• enumerate = to mention one by one; to count
• From Latin: 'enumerare' — to count out
• 'Enumerate the reasons', 'enumerated list' — going through them individually

quiz.question: He was an unregenerate optimist who never believed things could not improve. What does 'unregenerate' mean?
options: Stubbornly persisting in a quality despite pressure to change
Too committed to a belief to be influenced by evidence
Retaining a characteristic even when it caused problems
Maintaining an outlook regardless of what life had shown, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: Stubbornly persisting in a quality despite pressure to change
quiz.explanation:
Unregenerate means stubbornly persistent — refusing to change one's ways.
• unregenerate = stubbornly persistent; not reformed or converted
• From 'un-' + 'regenerate'
• 'An unregenerate sinner', 'unregenerate pessimist' — refusing to change

quiz.question: She was venerated as a pioneer who had opened the profession to women. What does 'venerate' mean?
options: To regard with deep respect and reverence
To honor publicly for a contribution made to a field
To hold in the highest regard for exceptional achievement
To treat as a figure worthy of the respect of an entire profession
quiz.answer: To regard with deep respect and reverence
quiz.explanation:
To venerate means to revere — to hold in the deepest respect and honor.
• venerate = to regard with great respect; to revere
• From Latin: 'venerari' — to worship
• 'Venerated figure', 'veneration of ancestors' — deep, reverential respect

quiz.question: The documents were incinerated to prevent them from falling into the wrong hands. What does 'incinerate' mean?
options: To destroy completely by burning
To burn confidential material to prevent its recovery
To reduce to ash through the application of fire
To use fire to dispose of materials that must not be found
quiz.answer: To destroy completely by burning
quiz.explanation:
To incinerate means to burn to ashes — to destroy completely by fire.
• incinerate = to destroy by burning
• From Latin: 'incinerare' — to reduce to ashes
• 'Incinerate waste', 'incinerator' — total destruction by fire

quiz.question: DNA evidence exonerated the man who had spent twenty years in prison. What does 'exonerate' mean?
options: To officially clear of blame or criminal responsibility
To release from prison after being found not guilty
To demonstrate through evidence that a conviction was wrong
To restore the reputation of someone wrongly imprisoned
quiz.answer: To officially clear of blame or criminal responsibility
quiz.explanation:
To exonerate means to clear — to free from blame or obligation.
• exonerate = to absolve; to officially clear of blame
• From Latin: 'exonerare' — to relieve of a burden
• 'Exonerated by DNA evidence' — the burden of guilt lifted

quiz.question: The contractor was fairly remunerated for his work on the project. What does 'remunerate' mean?
options: To pay for work or service — to compensate
To settle an invoice submitted by a contractor
To pay at a rate that reflected the value of the work done
To make payment on completion of an agreed scope of work
quiz.answer: To pay for work or service — to compensate
quiz.explanation:
To remunerate means to pay — to give compensation for work or service.
• remunerate = to pay for work or services
• From Latin: 'remunerari' — to reward
• 'Remuneration package', 'poorly remunerated' — payment for effort

quiz.question: The suspect refused to cooperate with police. What does 'cooperate' mean?
options: To work together or comply willingly
To provide information that would assist an investigation
To answer questions put by an investigating authority
To participate voluntarily in a process one is asked to join
quiz.answer: To work together or comply willingly
quiz.explanation:
To cooperate means to work together — to act jointly or comply willingly.
• cooperate = to work or act together for a common purpose
• From Latin: 'cooperari' — to work together
• 'Cooperate with authorities', 'cooperative venture' — acting in partnership

quiz.question: She spent two weeks in a convalescent home to recuperate after surgery. What does 'recuperate' mean?
options: To recover health or strength after illness
To rest in a supervised environment following an operation
To undergo rehabilitation to regain full physical function
To receive professional care during the recovery period
quiz.answer: To recover health or strength after illness
quiz.explanation:
To recuperate means to recover — to regain health and strength after illness.
• recuperate = to recover; to regain health or strength
• From Latin: 'recuperare' — to recover
• 'Recuperate from illness', 'recuperating slowly' — returning to full health

quiz.question: The senator vituperated against the opposition for an hour before being interrupted. What does 'vituperate' mean?
options: To speak with bitter, abusive criticism
To conduct a sustained verbal attack in a public forum
To criticize opponents in the most extreme possible language
To use parliamentary privilege to attack without restraint
quiz.answer: To speak with bitter, abusive criticism
quiz.explanation:
To vituperate means to abuse verbally — to speak with violent, bitter criticism.
• vituperate = to criticize harshly with abusive language
• From Latin: 'vituperare' — to find fault with
• 'Vituperative', 'vituperation' — abusive, sustained verbal attack

quiz.question: Friends called to commiserate with her after the disappointing result. What does 'commiserate' mean?
options: To express sympathy for someone's misfortune
To acknowledge a disappointment and show one cares
To share in the sadness of someone who has not succeeded
To let someone know they have support after a setback
quiz.answer: To express sympathy for someone's misfortune
quiz.explanation:
To commiserate means to sympathize — to share in and express sorrow at misfortune.
• commiserate = to express sympathy; to share grief
• From Latin: 'commiserari' — to pity together
• 'Commiserate with someone over a loss' — shared sorrow

quiz.question: He was an inveterate gambler who could not stop despite the consequences. What does 'inveterate' mean?
options: Having a habit or attitude too deeply established to change
So addicted to gambling that all other concerns came second
Continuing a destructive behavior despite its clear consequences
Unable to stop a habit regardless of the harm it caused
quiz.answer: Having a habit or attitude too deeply established to change
quiz.explanation:
Inveterate means deep-rooted — a habit or quality so long-established it cannot be changed.
• inveterate = firmly established; long-standing and habitual
• From Latin: 'inveterare' — to make old
• 'An inveterate liar', 'inveterate traveler' — the habit is fixed

quiz.question: The software developers iterated through several versions before releasing the final product. What does 'iterate' mean?
options: To repeat a process to improve on the previous version
To produce successive versions of a product through testing
To revise and refine a design repeatedly before release
To develop through multiple rounds of testing and improvement
quiz.answer: To repeat a process to improve on the previous version
quiz.explanation:
To iterate means to repeat — especially in a process of successive improvement.
• iterate = to repeat a procedure; to do again
• From Latin: 'iterare' — to repeat
• 'Iterate on a design' = refine through repeated cycles

quiz.question: The manager reiterated that the deadline was non-negotiable. What does 'reiterate' mean?
options: To say or do something again for emphasis
To make clear through repetition that a position would not change
To restate a requirement that had previously been communicated
To confirm a decision that was already known to all parties
quiz.answer: To say or do something again for emphasis
quiz.explanation:
To reiterate means to repeat — to say again, usually for emphasis.
• reiterate = to say or do again; to repeat for emphasis
• From Latin: 'reiterare' — to repeat
• 'Reiterate a point' = make it again, leaving no room for misunderstanding

quiz.question: Only half the adult population was literate at the time of independence. What does 'literate' mean?
options: Able to read and write
Educated to the level required for full participation in society
Having the basic skills taught in elementary schooling
Capable of reading simple texts and writing basic sentences
quiz.answer: Able to read and write
quiz.explanation:
Literate means able to read and write — having basic reading and writing skills.
• literate = able to read and write; knowledgeable in a field
• From Latin: 'litteratus' — learned
• 'Literate society', 'computer literate' — able to read, write, or navigate

quiz.question: The tribe's preliterate tradition preserved history entirely through oral storytelling. What does 'preliterate' mean?
options: Relating to a culture that does not have a written tradition
Using oral rather than written methods to preserve knowledge, as the evidence showed
Belonging to a period before writing was developed
Dependent on memory and speech because writing was unknown
quiz.answer: Relating to a culture that does not have a written tradition
quiz.explanation:
Preliterate means without a written tradition — existing before or without writing.
• preliterate = relating to a society that has not developed writing
• From 'pre-' + 'literate'
• 'Preliterate societies' pass knowledge through oral tradition

quiz.question: The poet alliterated the opening line with four words beginning with 'w'. What does 'alliterate' mean?
options: To use the same initial sound in closely connected words
To begin several words with the same letter for effect
To create a sound pattern by repeating consonants
To use repetition of opening sounds in a line of poetry, as the evidence showed
quiz.answer: To use the same initial sound in closely connected words
quiz.explanation:
To alliterate means to use alliteration — repeating the same initial sound.
• alliterate = to use the same initial sound in neighboring words
• From Latin: 'ad-' + 'littera' (letter)
• 'Peter Piper picked' = alliterates on 'p'

quiz.question: The olive oil had been adulterated with cheaper vegetable oil. What does 'adulterate' mean?
options: To make impure by adding inferior or foreign substances
To reduce the quality of a product by blending it with substitutes
To substitute part of a premium product with a cheaper alternative
To change the composition of a food product without disclosure
quiz.answer: To make impure by adding inferior or foreign substances
quiz.explanation:
To adulterate means to corrupt by adding inferior material — to debase by mixing.
• adulterate = to debase by adding inferior substances
• From Latin: 'adulterare' — to corrupt
• 'Adulterated food', 'adulterated evidence' — corrupted by foreign addition

quiz.question: The new software integrates with all existing systems. What does 'integrate' mean?
options: To combine into a whole — to bring parts together smoothly
To link to external systems without requiring modification, as the evidence showed
To connect to other platforms through standard interfaces
To work alongside existing technology without conflict
quiz.answer: To combine into a whole — to bring parts together smoothly
quiz.explanation:
To integrate means to combine into a whole — to unify separate parts.
• integrate = to make into a whole; to incorporate
• From Latin: 'integrare' — to make whole
• 'Integrate into society', 'integrate systems' — joining to form a unified whole

quiz.question: The wooden pier had disintegrated after years of neglect. What does 'disintegrate' mean?
options: To break apart into fragments — to fall to pieces
To decay to the point where the structure could not be saved
To collapse from rot that had spread through the wood
To weaken to the point where repair was no longer possible
quiz.answer: To break apart into fragments — to fall to pieces
quiz.explanation:
To disintegrate means to fall apart — to break into small pieces.
• disintegrate = to break apart into constituent parts
• From 'dis-' + 'integrate'
• 'Disintegrate on re-entry', 'the alliance disintegrated' — breaking into pieces

quiz.question: Her grandparents emigrated from Poland in the 1920s. What does 'emigrate' mean?
options: To leave one's own country to settle permanently elsewhere
To travel to another country in search of better prospects
To move abroad and take up permanent or long-term residence
To leave a country with the intention of not returning
quiz.answer: To leave one's own country to settle permanently elsewhere
quiz.explanation:
To emigrate means to leave — to move out of one's home country.
• emigrate = to leave one's country to settle elsewhere
• From Latin: 'emigrare' — to move away
• 'Emigrate from' (leave) vs. 'immigrate to' (arrive)

quiz.question: He denigrated her work in public while privately admiring it. What does 'denigrate' mean?
options: To unfairly criticize or belittle — to put down
To deny publicly the qualities one recognized privately
To misrepresent someone's achievements to reduce their reputation
To speak dismissively of someone in a professional setting
quiz.answer: To unfairly criticize or belittle — to put down
quiz.explanation:
To denigrate means to belittle unfairly — to attack someone's reputation.
• denigrate = to belittle; to speak disparagingly of
• From Latin: 'denigrare' — to blacken
• 'Denigrate someone's achievements' = unfairly diminish them

quiz.question: She called him an ingrate after he failed to thank anyone who had helped him. What is an ingrate?
options: An ungrateful person
Someone who takes help without acknowledging it
A person who shows no appreciation for favors received
Someone too self-absorbed to notice what others have done
quiz.answer: An ungrateful person
quiz.explanation:
An ingrate is an ungrateful person — one who fails to show gratitude.
• ingrate = an ungrateful person
• From Latin: 'ingratus' — ungrateful
• 'You ingrate!' — calling someone out for their ingratitude

quiz.question: Customers were irate about the unexplained delay. What does 'irate' mean?
options: Extremely angry
Frustrated enough to make formal complaints
Upset enough to threaten canceling their accounts
Annoyed in a way that made them vocal about their dissatisfaction
quiz.answer: Extremely angry
quiz.explanation:
Irate means very angry — furious and expressing it.
• irate = extremely angry
• From Latin: 'iratus' — made angry
• 'An irate customer', 'irate response' — the anger is intense and visible

quiz.question: Could you elaborate on what you mean by that? What does 'elaborate' mean here?
options: To explain in more detail — to expand on what was said
To clarify a point that others had found ambiguous
To provide supporting evidence for a claim already made
To go into more depth than was covered in an initial response
quiz.answer: To explain in more detail — to expand on what was said
quiz.explanation:
To elaborate means to expand — to explain in more detail.
• elaborate = to develop or present in more detail
• From Latin: 'elaborare' — to work out
• 'Elaborate on a point', 'elaborate design' — either expanding or complex in detail

quiz.question: A second witness corroborated the account of what had happened. What does 'corroborate' mean?
options: To support or confirm with additional evidence
To independently verify the account of another person
To provide testimony that agreed with an earlier statement
To strengthen a case by adding a second reliable account
quiz.answer: To support or confirm with additional evidence
quiz.explanation:
To corroborate means to confirm — to strengthen with supporting evidence.
• corroborate = to confirm; to give additional support to
• From Latin: 'corroborare' — to strengthen
• 'Corroborating evidence', 'corroborate a story' — independent confirmation
